data.title: Air Products gains on Q1 profit beat
data.description:
** Shares of industrial gas manufacturer Air Products rise 5.1% to $269.14

** Co beats Q1 profit estimates, helped by lower costs and higher sales in Europe and the Americas

** Posts Q1 adjusted profit of $3.16 per share vs. analysts' estimates of $3.05 per share - data compiled by LSEG

** Forecasts Q2 adjusted profit between $2.95 and $3.10 per share, the mid-point of which is in line with analysts' estimates of $3.02 per share

** In 2025, APD fell 14.8%

data.title: TotalEnergies, Galp Reaffirm Development Of Namibia Energy
data.description:
LA DEFENSE (dpa-AFX) - TotalEnergies SE (TTE), an energy company, Friday said that its Chief Executive Officer, Patrick Pouyanne along with energy company Galp's Chairman, Paula Amorim held a meeting with President of the Republic of Namibia, Netumbo Nandi-Ndaitwah.

In this meeting, they asserted their confidence in Namibia as a future oil and gas producing country and reaffirmed their commitment to develop the country's energy resources responsibly and updated the president on the transaction and the operatorship transition.

This meeting follows the agreement under which TotalEnergies will become operator of Petroleum Exploration License or PEL 83, home to Mopane discoveries while Galp will enter PEL 56 and PEL 91, home to the Venus discovery.

In pre-market activity, TTE shares were trading at $72.59, down 0.03% on the New York Stock Exchange.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Mergers and acquisitions
data.description:
The following bids, mergers, acquisitions and disposals were reported by 1430 GMT on Friday:

** Britain's Pinewood Technologies surged as much as 28% after the automotive service provider said it was set to agree to a 575.5 million pounds ($792.06 million) deal with Apax Partners if the private equity firm tables a formal offer.

** German media group Bertelsmann's <BTGGg.F> BMG Rights Management is considering a cash-and-stock offer for music company Concord in a deal that could be valued as high as $7 billion, Bloomberg News reported, citing sources.

** Aluminium Corporation of China (Chinalco) <601600.SS> and Rio Tinto <RIO.AX> agreed to acquire a controlling stake in Companhia Brasileira de Aluminio <CBAV3.SA> for 4.69 billion reais ($903.61 million), the firms said.

** Elon Musk's SpaceX is considering a potential merger with Tesla <TSLA.O> as well as an alternative combination with artificial-intelligence company xAI, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

** Australia's Nine Entertainment <NEC.AX> said it will buy digital outdoor media group QMS for A$850 million ($599.08 million) while selling its radio arm and shifting regional TV to an affiliate, accelerating a pivot to higher-growth digital assets.

** Apple <AAPL.O> said it has acquired Q.ai, an Israeli startup working on artificial intelligence technology for audio.

data.title: Wholesale Prices Jumped in December — WSJ
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

Prices charged by U.S. producers for their goods and services grew by more than expected in December.

  • The producer-price index increased by 0.5% last month, after rising by 0.2% in November, the Labor Department said.
  • Analysts polled by The Wall Street Journal were expecting a 0.3% increase.
  • Wholesale prices rose 3% overall in 2025.

The data will likely do little to shape investors' understanding of the economy, given continued delays in the publication calendar caused by the fall's government shutdown. But some PPI data feeds into the Commerce Department's formula for calculating the personal-consumption expenditures price index, the Federal Reserve's favored inflation metric.

The PCE inflation report is running nearly a month behind schedule, though. December's report won't land until late February, when economists will already have January consumer-inflation data on hand.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Imperial Oil dips on fourth-quarter profit fall
data.description:
** Shares of Canadian oil producer Imperial Oil down ~4% at C$137.22

** Company posts a fall in fourth-quarter profit, hurt by lower production and a drop in crude prices

** Company reports net income of C$492 million ($364.31 million), or C$1.00 per share, down from C$1.23 billion, or C$2.37 per share, last year

** Co's upstream production for the quarter was 444,000 gross barrels of oil equivalent per day (boepd), lower than 460,000 gross boepd a year earlier

** IMO's total throughput volumes fell to 408,000 barrels per day during the fourth quarter from 411,000 bpd a year ago

** In 2025, IMO was up ~34%

data.title: Trump Picks Kevin Warsh as Next Fed Chair; Dow Slips — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

Stocks traded lower early Friday, after investors finally learned who is due to be the next Federal Reserve chair.

After a monthslong selection process, President Trump has nominated Kevin Warsh, a former Fed governor-turned-critic, to chair the central bank.

"I have known Kevin for a long period of time, and have no doubt that he will go down as one of the GREAT Fed Chairmen, maybe the best," Trump said in a social-media post.

Major indexes slipped 0.5% or less. The dollar gained and longer-term bond yields rose.

Some market participants see Warsh as a relatively safe choice, given his Fed experience and his track record as a hawk, or supporter of tight monetary policy. That could make him more resistant to calls from the administration to slash interest rates.

However, Warsh has recently contended that AI and deregulation could help hold down inflation. He also wants the Fed to have a much smaller balance sheet.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Sandisk's stock gets 'one of the most delayed upgrades in history' after blowout earnings
data.description:
By Britney Nguyen

Analysts say Sandisk might wind up being sold out of storage products for years due to a widening gulf between supply and demand

Sandisk shares were soaring Friday morning.

Missed out on Sandisk's 1,400% stock rally? The company's blockbuster earnings suggest to analysts that there's more room for shares to run higher.

Sandisk (SNDK) is benefiting from booming storage demand even more than Wall Street was expecting. The company's quarterly revenue guidance beat expectations by almost 60% at the midpoint and its profit outlook surpassed estimates by 150%.

That strength in storage demand led Raymond James analyst Melissa Fairbanks to issue what she called "arguably one of the most delayed upgrades in history," as she moved to an outperform rating on Sandisk's stock.

Fairbanks sees more opportunities for upside, "not surprisingly driven by the demand and pricing implications" of the unprecedented AI data-center buildout.

Her new $725 price target implies 34% upside from Thursday's $539.30 closing price. Sandisk's stock was up about 23% ahead of the market open on Friday morning.

See more: Sandisk's eye-popping earnings forecast helps justify 1,400% stock surge

The Raymond James team sees strong pricing power for Sandisk going forward as demand could vastly outstrip supply "to the point of [Sandisk] potentially being sold out for years," they said. New supply will likely be limited in the near term, they added.

While suppliers of dynamic random-access memory like Micron Technology (MU) have received a lot of love lately, Fairbanks said the demand environment for NAND memory looks sustainable too, and Sandisk stands to be "a key beneficiary" of the shift of storage supply to data centers and away from consumer devices.

Jefferies analyst Blayne Curtis also sees a widening gap between NAND supply and demand.

"This NAND cycle has been nothing short of shocking," Curtis said in a Thursday note. While a downcycle is expected eventually, Curtis said he sees "no clear end in sight" and that the current supply-demand dynamic will likely fall "even more out of balance."

Sandisk's earnings report was "the first real look into the magnitude of structural imbalance" of supply and demand in the NAND market since prices started going higher in the fall, he said.

He expects bit growth, or an increase in digital storage capacity in the market, to be in the mid-teens range as hyperscalers lock in long-term agreements at least through 2028.

That the increasing demand for storage is due to inference, or the process of running AI models which Curtis said contributes to hyperscaler revenue, is another "layer of sustainability to the story."

Don't miss: Here's how Sandisk's stock can follow its 1,000% surge with even more gains

Mizuho trading-desk analyst Jordan Klein noted that while Sandisk has been popular with retail traders, it also seems to be attracting attention from "real active managers who see the fundamentals accelerating and also need to chase to keep up with sector or benchmark performance."

-Britney Nguyen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Collapse of Eutelsat's Ground Stations Sale Likely Due to Regulatory Issues — Market Talk
data.description: While French satellite operator Eutelsat's failure to complete the sale of its ground stations might be disappointing to some investors, Deutsche Bank analysts say the situation has changed since it first announced the deal in August 2024. Eutelsat completed a 1.5 billion euro fundraising in June and that should make upcoming refinancing easier, they add. Eutelsat didn't disclose why the deal collapsed but the analysts believe it was most likely regulatory issues given that all parties had agreed to the deal. Still, the analysts say there is further infrastructure deal potential, given interest in the satellite sector. Eutelsat shares are down 4.3% at 2.25 euros.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Colgate-Palmolive Says Uncertainty Will Continue in 2026 — Market Talk
data.description: Colgate-Palmolive issued a wider-than-normal outlook for the year to account for all the uncertainty, CEO Noel Wallace says on a call with analysts. "The geopolitical environment, including tariffs, is volatile, particularly in Latin America, and the U.S. market remains sluggish," he says. "While we think trends will improve, we're not building in a big rebound because of this uncertainty." The toothpaste and soap maker says it is still well positioned to grow in 2026, though, citing its new operating strategy and the strength and resilience of its model. Colgate-Palmolive climbs 5% in early trading. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Baltic Dry Index at Over 1-Month High, Posts Monthly Gain
data.description:
The Baltic Exchange’s dry bulk index, which tracks rates for vessels transporting dry commodities, soared about 7.3% to the highest since mid-December at 2,148 points on Friday.

The capesize index, which typically transports 150,000-ton cargoes such as iron ore and coal, jumped 12% to 3,507 points; and the panamax index, which usually carries 60,000-70,000 tons of coal or grain, rose 1.6% to 1,743 points.

Among smaller vessels, the supramax index added five points, or 0.5%, to 1,067 points.

The benchmark index advanced 6.7% for the month and recorded a 27% weekly gain, notching its third consecutive weekly rise.

The Code can be viewed on the Panel's website at www.thetakeoverpanel.org.uk.

data.title: Disney Hires Morgan Stanley's Swinburne to Lead IR, Corporate Strategy
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

Disney has hired Morgan Stanley analyst Benjamin Swinburne as the entertainment giant's new executive vice president of investor relations and corporate strategy.

Disney on Friday said Swinburne, most recently managing director and head of U.S. media and telecom & cable services research at Morgan Stanley, will join the Burbank, Calif., company in the near future.

Swinburne, who joined Morgan Stanley as a research analyst in 1999, will report directly to Hugh Johnston, Disney's chief financial officer, the company said.

Disney said Swinburne will lead communication with its investors, sell-side analysts and other key stakeholders and will also oversee the company's long-term strategic planning and market analysis, identifying growth opportunities based on industry trends and evolving entertainment consumption.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com
